attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to his General Practice clinic with a two-day history of severe lower back pain after lifting a heavy chair at home. He rates the pain as 8/10 initially, improving to 4/10 but not resolving completely. The pain is dull and aching, with sharp spasms on certain movements. He has experienced multiple similar episodes before, most recently 1 week ago, and before that, 1-2 monthly for several years. He sees a physiotherapist at times for his back pain with some benefit.

The pain is localised to the right lumbar area without radiation. There are no red flags such as weight loss, recent infections, chills or fevers. Over-the-counter analgesics provide only slight relief. He works in a stressful job in management at a construction site, often sitting at a desk for over 8 hours per day.

On examination, he appears distressed. His pulse rate is 88/min and blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg. Musculoskeletal examination reveals paraspinal tenderness and spasm in the right lumbar region. Straight leg raise is negative bilaterally. Neurological lower limb examination is normal. He is able to transfer from sitting to standing with some difficulty due to pain. His gait is antalgic but stable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate long term step in management?
options: Recommend early return to moderate-intensity exercise
Suggest back brace for the first week
attributes.explanation:
This patient has presented with an acute flare of recurrent lower back pain with significant psychosocial impact. Pain is a result of complex interactions between biological, psychological and social factors, which is why no individual patients experience pain the same way. Utilisation of the biopsychosocial model allows General Practitioners to address these factors, which has been shown to improve patients' functional capacity and satisfaction more than a purely biomedical approach. A wide range of psychosocial variables impact the probability of developing chronic pain, so these factors must always be addressed.

In this case acute management of this patients back pain may include judicious use of analgesics such as paracetamol and anti-inflammatories and recommendations for gentle exercise and physiotherapy. However, several yellow flags are present in this case that increase the risk of chronic pain developing, including recurrent episodes, significant functional impairment, high levels of distress, and a stressful sedentary job. Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is an evidence-based treatment that can help address maladaptive beliefs and behaviours around pain. It is particularly useful when simple measures like physiotherapy and analgesia are insufficient. Referral to a psychologist for CBT is the most appropriate step for definitive management here.

Early return to moderate-intensity exercise is not advisable. Current evidence supports staying active and slowly increasing physical activity for acute low back pain, starting with low-intensity activity and gradually progressing. Low-impact aerobic exercise with emphasis on core muscles has shown beneficial results. Early return to moderate or high intensity exercise can increase the risk of injury.

A back brace is sometimes used in the acute phase but evidence for its effectiveness is lacking. Braces are not routinely recommended and may promote unhelpful fear-avoidance beliefs if used unnecessarily and possible prolonged wearing of an orthosis weakens the trunk of stabilizing muscles. Benefit from wearing orthotic braces seems to be greatest in degenerative diseases and post vertebral fracture or in those with metastases to lumbar spine.

Oxycodone is a potent opioid that carries risks of misuse, abuse and dependence, especially in patients with chronic or recurrent pain. Opioids are not first-line for musculoskeletal pain and should be avoided if possible. If prescribed, the lowest effective dose of short-acting opioids for a limited duration is recommended.

Refer for spinal MRI is not indicated in this case. The patient has no red flag features such as progressive neurological deficit, cauda equina syndrome symptoms, fever, unexplained weight loss, or history of malignancy that would warrant urgent imaging. Current guidelines recommend against routine imaging for non-specific low back pain as it does not improve outcomes and may lead to unnecessary interventions. Imaging should be reserved for cases where red flags are present or when conservative management has failed and surgical intervention is being considered.
learningPoints: Pain is a result of complex interactions between biological, psychological and social factors, hence why no individual patients experience pain the same way.
As pain is a biopsychosocial issue, a biopsychosocial approach to treatment is essential.

Prescription of opioids should be avoided unless absolutely necessary in back pain, and if so should be prescribed as short-acting doses and for a limited duration to mitigate the risk of misuse and abuse.
Education, reassurance, hot or cold therapy, and exercise are all pillars of non-pharmacological treatment of back pain.
references: Trachsel LA, Cascella M. Pain Theory. [Updated 2021 Aug 2]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK545194/
Meints SM, Edwards RR., 2018. Evaluating psychosocial contributions to chronic pain outcomes. Prog Neuropsychopharmacol Biol Psychiatry, 87(Pt B):168-182. doi:10.1016/j.pnpbp.2018.01.017

Strudwick, K. et al., 2018. Review article: Best practice management of low back pain in the emergency department (part 1 of the musculoskeletal injuries rapid review series). Emergency medicine Australasia, 30(1), pp.18–35.

Schott C, Zirke S, Schmelzle JM, Kaiser C, Fernández LAI. Effectiveness of lumbar orthoses in low back pain: Review of the literature and our results. Orthop Rev (Pavia). 2018;10(4):7791. Published 2018 Dec 6. doi:10.4081/or.2018.7791
